title: A Família e os Possessivos
subtitle: The Family & Possessive Determiners
description: Learn family vocabulary, possessive determiners and pronouns, and regular -er verb conjugations.
topics: Possessives (o meu, o teu, o seu...)

vocabulary.example: O meu pai é indiano. A minha mãe é portuguesa.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: My father is Indian. My mother is Portuguese.
vocabulary.tip: The plural is irregular: os pais (parents can also mean 'fathers' in context), as mães

vocabulary.example: Eles têm dois filhos: um filho e uma filha.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: They have two children: a son and a daughter.
vocabulary.tip: os filhos = children (mixed or male group); as filhas = daughters

vocabulary.example: Eu não tenho irmãos. A Patrícia tem uma irmã e um irmão.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I don't have siblings. Patricia has a sister and a brother.
vocabulary.tip: os irmãos can mean siblings in general; as irmãs = sisters

vocabulary.example: Os meus avós moram em Lisboa.
vocabulary.tip: The plural is special: avós (grandparents), not a combined -ôs form

vocabulary.example: Eles têm dois netos e três netas.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: They have two grandsons and three granddaughters.
vocabulary.tip: o/neto/a and avô/avó follow the same -o/-a pattern

vocabulary.example: O meu tio mora em Faro. A minha tia é professora.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: My uncle lives in Faro. My aunt is a teacher.

vocabulary.example: Eu tenho muitos primos e primas.
O Paulo é o marido da Patrícia.
vocabulary.tip: After SER, no article: 'É casado com...' (married to...)

vocabulary.example: A Mónica é a sobrinha do Paulo.
Os sogros da Patrícia são o Carlos e a Sara.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Patricia's parents-in-law are Carlos and Sara.
vocabulary.tip: In-law relationships are formed with compostos: sogro/a, genro/ nora

vocabulary.example: O marido da Ana é o Luís, é o meu cunhado.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Ana's husband is Luís, he is my brother-in-law.
vocabulary.tip: The 'nh' is key to being understood

vocabulary.word: os meus pais / os meus avós
vocabulary.pronunciation: oosh may-oosh PA-yeesh / oosh may-oosh ah-VOSH
vocabulary.example: Os meus pais são portugueses. Os meus avós são do Algarve.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: My parents are Portuguese. My grandparents are from the Algarve.
vocabulary.tip: Possessives agree with the THING POSSESSED, not with the possessor

vocabulary.word: O meu / A minha / O teu / A tua
vocabulary.pronunciation: oo may-oo / ah mee-NYAH / oo t-oo / ah too-ah
vocabulary.example: O meu pai é alto. A minha mãe é alta.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: My father is tall. My mother is tall.
vocabulary.tip: In Portuguese, possessives are preceded by the definite article: o meu livro (not just 'meu livro')

vocabulary.word: O seu / A sua / dele / dela
vocabulary.pronunciation: oo SAY-oo / ah SOO-ah / DEH-leh / DEH-lah
vocabulary.example: O pai da Maria = O pai dela. É o seu pai.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Maria's father = Her father. It's her father.
vocabulary.tip: 'Dele'/'dela' = of him/of her. 'Seu'/'sua' can mean his/her/your — context clarifies!

sections.heading: Family Vocabulary & Gender Patterns
sections.body: Portuguese family nouns follow predictable gender patterns. Most family members have distinct masculine and feminine forms. The key patterns are: (1) -o/-a: filho/filha, primo/prima, tio/tia. (2) -ô/-ó: avô/avó (irregular plural: avós). (3) -o/-a with different roots: pai/mãe, irmão/irmã. When referring to a mixed group, use the masculine plural: 'os filhos' can mean children (boys and girls), 'os irmãos' can mean siblings.

sections.heading: Possessive Determiners (Determinantes Possessivos)
sections.body: Possessive determiners in Portuguese indicate ownership and agree in gender and number with the THING POSSESSED, not with the possessor. This is different from English! For example: 'o meu pai' (my father) — 'meu' is masculine because 'pai' is masculine, regardless of whether the speaker is male or female. Another key difference: Portuguese possessives are preceded by the definite article: 'o meu livro' (not just 'meu livro'). The article is dropped only in very specific cases like after the verb SER when identifying: 'Este livro é meu' (This book is mine).

sections.heading: Possessive Pronouns (dele, dela, deles, delas)
sections.body: After prepositions like 'de' (of), Portuguese uses special possessive forms: dele (of him/his), dela (of her/hers), deles (of them — m.), delas (of them — f.). These are formed by combining 'de' + the personal pronoun. For example: 'O pai da Maria' = 'O pai dela' (Maria's father = Her father). These forms are essential for avoiding repetition and are used constantly in spoken Portuguese.

sections.heading: Possessive Pronouns After 'de'
rows: O carro dele é azul. (His car is blue.)
A casa dela é grande. (Her house is big.)
Os filhos deles são simpáticos. (Their children are nice.)
As amigas delas moram aqui. (Their friends live here.)

items.portuguese: O meu pai é indiano e a minha mãe é portuguesa.
items.translation: My father is Indian and my mother is Portuguese.
items.pronunciation: oo may-oo pay eh een-dee-AH-noo ee ah mee-NYAH may eh poor-too-GAY-zah.

items.portuguese: O pai da Maria é médico. = O pai dele é médico.
items.translation: Maria's father is a doctor. = Her father is a doctor.
items.pronunciation: oo pay dah mah-REE-ah eh MEH-dee-koo. oo pay DEH-leh eh MEH-dee-koo.

items.portuguese: Os nossos avós moram em Lisboa.
items.pronunciation: oosh NOH-soosh ah-VOSH MOH-rahm eem LEESH-boh-ah.

sections.heading: Regular -er Verbs (2nd Conjugation)
sections.body: The second major group of Portuguese verbs ends in -er. Like -ar verbs, most -er verbs are regular and follow a predictable pattern. To conjugate, remove the -er ending to get the stem, then add: -o (eu), -es (tu), -e (ele/ela), -emos (nós), -em (eles/elas). Notice the endings are very similar to -ar verbs, except the first person singular ends in -o (not -o... same!) and the plural endings are -emos/-em instead of -amos/-am. The key difference: -er verb stems often have a 'c' or 'ç' that changes spelling to preserve pronunciation.

sections.heading: Regular -er Conjugation Pattern (using COMER as model)
Common Mistakes & Tips
items: Possessives agree with the thing possessed, NOT the possessor: 'o meu mãe' is WRONG — it's 'a minha mãe' because 'mãe' is feminine.
Don't forget the definite article before possessives: 'o meu pai' (not 'meu pai'). The article is only dropped in specific cases like 'É meu'.
Watch out for 'seu/sua' ambiguity — it can mean 'his', 'her', or 'your'. Use 'dele/dela' to be clear: 'O carro dele' (his car).
For -er verbs, the 'nós' form ends in -emos (comemos), not -imos. That's only for -ir verbs.
The verb 'viver' (to live) is an -er verb, while 'morar' (to live/reside) is an -ar verb. Both are correct in European Portuguese.
Family plural: 'os filhos' can mean 'the children' (mixed group) or 'the sons'. Context tells you which.
When describing your family, use SER for identity and TER for possession: 'Tenho dois irmãos' (I have two brothers), 'O meu irmão é alto' (My brother is tall).

exercises.title: Choose the Correct Possessive
exercises.instructions: Select the correct possessive determiner for each sentence.

items.question: ___ pai é médico. (eu)
items.explanation: Possessives need the definite article: 'O meu pai'. 'Meu' without the article is only used in specific cases.

items.question: ___ mãe é professora. (tu)
items.explanation: 'A tua mãe' — 'tua' agrees with 'mãe' (feminine). The article 'a' is required.

items.question: ___ avós moram em Lisboa. (nós)
items.explanation: 'Os nossos avós' — 'nossos' agrees with 'avós' (masculine plural). First person plural possessive.

items.question: O pai da Ana é alto. = O pai ___ é alto.
items.explanation: 'Dela' = de + ela. 'O pai dela' = Her father. We use 'dela' to replace 'da Ana'.

exercises.title: Complete with the Correct Possessive
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank with the correct possessive determiner.

exercises.title: Conjugate the -er Verb
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct conjugation for each sentence.

items.question: Eu ___ peixe. (comer)
items.explanation: Eu + stem + o = como. First person singular of regular -er verbs ends in -o.

items.question: Tu ___ café? (beber)
items.explanation: Tu + stem + es = bebes. Second person singular of regular -er verbs ends in -es.

items.question: Nós ___ português. (aprender)
items.explanation: Nós + stem + emos = aprendemos. First person plural of regular -er verbs ends in -emos.

items.question: Eles ___ em Lisboa. (viver)
items.explanation: Eles + stem + em = vivem. Third person plural of regular -er verbs ends in -em.

exercises.title: Build Sentences with -er Verbs
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct verb form to complete each sentence.

items.hint: 1st person singular of ler (to read) — irregular but common
